coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of photoluminescent MoS2 and WS2 quantum dots with strong surface-state emission
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Vanthan
Dong, Qi
Yan, Lihe
Zhao, Na
Le, Phuoc Huu
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal dichalcogenides quantum dots (QDs), especially molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and tungsten disulfide (WS2) QDs, have attracted great attention as a potential candidate for the application of fluorescent materials. However, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of these QDs is still very low, which severely limits their availability for practical applications. Here, we present a simple and green strategy to synthesis highly photoluminescent MoS2/WS2 QDs using femtosecond laser ablation of raw MoS2/WS2 powders in solution. The as-prepared MoS2/WS2 QDs exhibit intense and excellent photostability photoluminescence in blue region with a satisfying PLQY of 22.5%. By detailed characterization and spectroscopic study, we find that abundant surface functional groups, such as C–O, CO, and C–N, can efficiently introduce new surface-state emission centers and result in their intense fluorescent emission. A simplified energy level and electron transition diagram has been proposed to help understand the surface-state emission mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanomaterials
Photoluminescence mechanism
Laser ablation
Surface functionalization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
Morphological properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
Optical properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
Chemical structures of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
Photoluminescence mechanism of MoS2 and WS2 QDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs. (a, b) TEM images of MoS2 and WS2 QDs prepared by femtosecond laser ablation in DEA, respectively. Insets are their corresponding size distributions, HRTEM images, and SAED patterns. (c, d) AFM images of MoS2 and WS2 QDs and the corresponding height profile along the line in the AFM image.

Optical properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs. (a) PL spectra of MoS2 QDs. Insets are photographs of the MoS2 QDs aqueous solution under visible light and UV irradiation (365 nm). (b) Emission spectra of MoS2 and WS2 QDs at 360 nm excitation. (c) Photostability test of MoS2 and WS2 QDs under UV light irradiation (365 nm, 8 W UV lamp). (d) UV-visible absorption spectra of MoS2 and WS2 QDs and PLE spectrum of MoS2 QDs detected at 405 nm.

Chemical structures of MoS2 and WS2 QDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of as-prepared MoS2 QDs: (a) Mo 3d, (b) S 2p. High-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of (c) MoS2 and (d) WS2 QDs, respectively. FTIR spectra of (e) MoS2 QDs and (f) WS2 QDs prepared by femtosecond laser ablation in DEA and water.

Time-resolved PL spectra of MoS2 QDs: at detected wavelengths of 380, 405, and 430 nm with (a) 300 nm excitation and (b) 343 nm excitation; (c) detected at 405 nm with excitation wavelengths of 273, 300, and 343 nm. (d) Schematics of the PL mechanism in MoS2 and WS2 QDs. S0, S1, and Ssur represent ground state, excited state of QDs core, and surface state, respectively.
Multiexponential fitting for time-resolved PL spectra of the MoS2 and WS2 QDs at detection wavelength of 405 nm when excited at 273, 300, and 343 nm.
Values of carrier relaxation parameters of the MoS2 and WS2 QDs at detection wavelength of 405 nm using the proposed kinetic model.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of photoluminescent MoS2 and WS2 quantum dots with strong surface-state emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Biophysics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, 179 Nguyen Van Cu Street, Can Tho, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Biophysics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, 179 Nguyen Van Cu Street, Can Tho, Viet Nam

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal dichalcogenides quantum dots (QDs), especially molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and tungsten disulfide (WS2) QDs, have attracted great attention as a potential candidate for the application of fluorescent materials. However, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of these QDs is still very low, which severely limits their availability for practical applications. Here, we present a simple and green strategy to synthesis highly photoluminescent MoS2/WS2 QDs using femtosecond laser ablation of raw MoS2/WS2 powders in solution. The as-prepared MoS2/WS2 QDs exhibit intense and excellent photostability photoluminescence in blue region with a satisfying PLQY of 22.5%. By detailed characterization and spectroscopic study, we find that abundant surface functional groups, such as C–O, CO, and C–N, can efficiently introduce new surface-state emission centers and result in their intense fluorescent emission. A simplified energy level and electron transition diagram has been proposed to help understand the surface-state emission mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Trigged by the great achievements on graphene, some other types of two-dimensional nanomaterials, such as monolayer hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have attracted great attention due to their unusual electronic, optical and mechanical properties [1–3]. TMDs such as molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and tungsten disulfide (WS2) have sizable bandgaps that change from indirect to direct in single layers [3–5]. The direct bandgap results in photoluminescence (PL) from monolayer MoS2/WS2 nanosheets and their PL can reach near-unity quantum yield after chemical treatment [6], which opens the possibility for many optoelectronic applications [4,7]. Moreover, when the lateral size of MoS2/WS2 nanosheets being reduced to be typical less than 20 nm, MoS2/WS2 quantum dots (QDs) could be obtained [8]. As compared with their native nanosheet forms, QDs offer higher special surface area and more edge atoms, which are beneficial to catalytic applications [9–11]. Besides, the QDs possess bright PL emission because of their stronger quantum confinement effect and ease to be functionalized make them promising materials in fluorescence sensing [12–15], bioimaging [16,17], and biomedical applications [18,19].

Although various methods have been developed to synthesize MoS2/WS2 nanosheets, few works were reported on the synthesis of MoS2/WS2 QDs, especially WS2 QDs. Several reports showed successful synthesis of MoS2/WS2 QDs using solvothermal/hydrothermal cutting of native exfoliated nanosheets [9,12], utralsonic treatment of 3D bulk raw material [7,16,17], and hydrothermal reaction of chemical precursors [13,14,20]. However, most of these methods suffer from complicated and long-time processes [10,16]. Most importantly, the prepared QDs exhibit low PL quantum yield (PLQY), typically less than 10%, which severely limits their availability for practical applications [12,14,17,20,21]. Therefore, it is of critical need to establish a simple method for synthesis of water-soluble and highly photoluminescent MoS2/WS2 QDs.

In this study, we present a simple and green strategy to synthesis MoS2 and WS2 QDs with strong PL using laser ablation method. The MoS2 and WS2 QDs were directly synthesized in aqueous phase by femtosecond laser ablation of raw MoS2/WS2 powders in diethylamine (DEA) solution. The as-prepared QDs exhibit intense and excellent photostability PL emission in blue region with a satisfying PLQY of 22.5%. By detailed characterization and spectroscopic study, it is proved concretely that the bright PL in the as-prepared QDs is originated from surface-state created by abundant functional groups on its surface, such as C–O, CO, and C–N. With the outstanding PL properties, these QDs show great potential applications in optoelectronic and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: MoS2 and WS2 QDs were synthesized via femtosecond laser ablation of MoS2 and WS2 powders in DEA, respectively. Typically, 20 mg of MoS2/WS2 powders, with size ~2 μm, was dispersed into 50 ml of DEA via ultrasonication. Next, ~10 ml of the suspension was put into a glass beaker for laser irradiation. The laser beam (Ti: sapphire femtosecond laser system, central wavelength: 800 nm, pulse duration: 150 fs, and repetition rate: 1 kHz) was focused into suspension at a laser fluence of 200 J/cm2 by 25 mm lens for 30 min. During the laser irradiation, a magnetic stirrer was used to prevent gravitational settling of the suspended powders. After laser irradiation, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min and the MoS2/WS2 QDs were obtained from the supernatant.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the MoS2 and WS2 QDs were obtained via a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (model FEI TITAN G2). A UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) was employed to measure the absorption spectra of the samples. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a VERTEX 70 (Bruker) using KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out on AXIS ULtrabld (Kratos) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The PL characterizations including emission spectra, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, and time-resolved PL spectra were recorded using FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh). For the time-resolved PL measurements, picosecond pulsed LED (central wavelengths: 273, 300, and 343 nm, pulse duration: < 850 ps, repetition rate: 10 MHz) was used as excitation sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared MoS2 and WS2 QDs were characterized by TEM, HRTEM, and AFM. As shown in the TEM images (Fig. 1 a and b), the QDs distribute homogeneously on the copper grid and no aggregation can be observed, indicating that the QDs are well dispersed in solution. The size distributions of the QDs are presented in the insets of Fig. 1a, b, and a narrow distribution in the range of 1–3 nm is observed. The average diameters of MoS2 and WS2 QDs are 1.8 and 1.5 nm, respectively. The size difference between MoS2 and WS2 QDs may be resulted from the interior property of MoS2 and WS2 unite cells [9]. The highly paralleled and ordered lattice fringes in HRTEM images (inserted in Fig. 1a and b) suggest MoS2 and WS2 QDs are well-crystallized. The lattice spacing of MoS2 QDs is 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (103) faces of MoS2 crystals. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of WS2 QDs is about 0.205 nm, which matches well with the (006) lattice of WS2 crystals. The structure of as-prepared QDs is further verified by selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) as shown in insets of Fig. 1a and b. The aureole in SAED patterns reveal that these QDs are polycrystalline, which are different from bulk MoS2 and WS2, suggesting that the destruction of MoS2 and WS2 powders during laser ablation process. The AFM images indicate the height of the QDs ranges from approximately 1.5 to 3.0 nm (Fig. 1 c, d), suggesting the as-prepared QDs are few-layered. The results reveal the successful synthesis of highly crystalline MoS2 and WS2 QDs with a narrow size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Steady-state fluorescence measurement was conducted for the QDs dispersed in aqueous solution. Under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, these QDs emit strong blue fluorescence as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 a. The PL emission spectra of MoS2 and WS2 QDs (Fig. 2a) show an excitation-independent property, which is similar with the emission properties of carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots [22–24]. The fluorescence peak at ~420 nm does not shift with increasing excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm and reach maximum at 360 nm excitation. Noticeably, the emission properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs are almost the same. The emissions of these QDs at 360 nm excitation are nearly overlapped (Fig. 2b). The PLQY of MoS2 and WS2 QDs in ethanol are calculated to be 22.5% and 21.8%, respectively (using quinine sulfate as the standard sample with excitation wavelength of 360 nm). The PLQY of as-prepared QDs are superior to the previously reported MoS2/WS2 QDs [10,12,17,20,21,25,26] and comparable to those reported previously for carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots [22,27,28]. Furthermore, the as-prepared MoS2 and WS2 QDs possess an excellent photostability. The QDs are photostable under long-time irradiation of UV light (UV lamp, 365 nm, 8 W) that the peak intensity remains ~93% after 4 h irradiation (Fig. 2c). With the high PLQY and excellent photostability, these QDs show great potential applications in optoelectronic and bioimaging.

The UV-visible absorption spectra of MoS2 and WS2 QDs also show nearly the same profile with two characteristic absorption bands (Fig. 2d). One is centered at around 275 nm with an absorption edge of ~320 nm, which is assigned to the excitonic features of these QDs [9,12]. The other absorption band is subtle and centered about 325 nm with a tail extending up to 400 nm. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of the QDs detected at 405 nm shows two evident peaks located at around 305 and 360 nm (Fig. 2d). The maximum peaks in PLE spectrum are just right in the region of two absorption bands in UV-visible absorption spectra indicating that these absorption bans is corresponding to the strong PL peak around 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structures of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to shed light on the origin of their intense PL property, the chemical compositions and structures of these QDs are further investigated using XPS and FTIR analysis. As shown in the high-resolution Mo 3d spectrum (Fig. 3 a), two characteristic peaks at 227.0 and 232.5 eV belong to Mo4+ 3d5/2 and Mo4+ 3d3/2, respectively. The S 2p3/2, and S 2p1/2 peaks of MoS2 QDs are observed at 161.4 and 163.5 eV (Fig. 2 b), and a peak located around 167.6 eV is ascribed to S6+, which might originate from the oxidation of S edges during the laser ablation process. Besides Mo/W and S elements, C, N, and O elements also exist in the surface of these QDs. High-resolution C 1s spectrum of MoS2 QDs (Fig. 3c) reveal the presence of CC (284.6 eV), C–H (283.4 eV), C–O/C–N (285.9 eV), and CO (287.9 eV). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of WS2 QDs (Fig. 3d) also shows the presence of CC, C–O/C–N, and CO bonds. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 3e and f) of the QDs further confirm that abundant surface functional groups are created on the surfaces of the QDs. For example, there are stretching vibrations of N–H bond at 3360 cm−1, C–H bond at 2920 and 2850 cm−1, bending vibrations of CH2 at 1470 cm−1, and vibrational absorption band of CO bond at 1630 cm−1. Remarkably, the category and quantity of surface functional groups in MoS2 and WS2 QDs are almost similar with the dominance of C–H, CO, and N–H bonds. For formation mechanism of these QDs, femtosecond laser ablation can creates a high-temperature and high-pressure plasma plume that result in the dramatically chemical reaction between raw materials (MoS2 or WS2) and DEA solution [29,30]. Subsequently, abundant functional groups are created on the surface of these QDs.

To study the effects of surface structure to their PL properties, pristine MoS2 and WS2 QDs with less surface groups are synthesized by laser ablation in water. Although the MoS2/WS2 QDs synthesized in water possess similar core-structures with QDs prepared in DEA, there is basically no detectable PL from these pristine QDs. The FTIR spectra of pristine QDs exhibit different category and smaller amount of functional groups compared with that of MoS2/WS2 QDs prepared in DEA (Fig. 3e and f). The surface groups in the pristine MoS2 and WS2 QDs are almost similar, in which the O–H and C–O bonds are dominant with small quantity. The results indicate that there is a strong correlation between surface chemical structure of QDs and their PL properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence mechanism of MoS2 and WS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To gain an insight into the PL mechanism of these QDs, the PL dynamic of the QDs are further carried out. The PL dynamics of MoS2 QDs detected around the fluorescence peaks are monitored with excitation wavelengths of 273, 300, and 343 nm. For all of excitation wavelengths, the PL dynamic of MoS2 QDs reveals nearly invariant for changing the emission wavelength (Fig. 4 a and b), which is similar with previous report of diethylenetriamine-doped QDs [25]. Each decay curve is fitted with a double-exponential function; the best-fit parameters and average lifetimes are listed in Table 1 . The PL dynamics of MoS2 QDs exhibit two distinct relaxation time scales, fast (~1 ns) and slow (~7 ns) decays, for 300 nm excitation (Fig. 4a). At 343 nm excitation, the fast decay of carrier recombination in the QDs becomes increasingly dominant that the percentage of the fast component of the total fluorescence intensity is 98% (Fig. 4b). The PL dynamics detected at the fluorescence peak of 405 nm at different wavelength excitations are shown in Fig. 4c. The slow decays are gradually weakened by increasing excitation wavelength from 273 to 343 nm and only fast decays exist in the decay curves at excitations of 343 nm. The results suggest two different pathways of electron-hole radiative recombination, each with a distinct relaxation time scales, exist in the PL emission process of QDs. Interestingly, similar evolutions of PL dynamics are also observed in WS2 QDs, and the obtained fitting parameters of WS2 QDs are approximate to that of MoS2 QDs (Table 1).

Here, we propose a mechanism to explain the intense PL in the MoS2 and WS2 QDs. The aforementioned results prove that the intense PL in MoS2 and WS2 QDs is attributed to the presence of special surface functional groups. Some previous reports showed that edge defects on MoS2 and WS2 nanosheets can induce nonradiative recombination resulting in the drastic decrease of their PLQY [31]. In this work, abundant functional groups are created on the surface of MoS2 and WS2 QDs during the laser ablation process. These surface functional groups can suppress the nonradiative recombination and enhance their PL emission [32]. Importantly, the surface functional groups such as C–O, CO, C–N can form a surface-state energy level and become an isolated emission center on the surface site [33–35]. The formation of these surface states is confirmed by the extending to visible region of UV-visible absorption spectra of QDs [36,37]. Therefore, the surface emission mechanism in MoS2 and WS2 QDs in this study is similar with that in carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots [33,34]. Since all functional groups may form different emission centers, the as-prepared QDs exhibit excitation-independent PL properties could be attributed to the dominance of one emission centers. Furthermore, the MoS2 and WS2 QDs possess similar surface structure and therefore exhibit the similar PL properties.

For carrier dynamics mechanism, the fast and slow decays could correspond to direct excitation-recombination of carriers on the surface states and a relaxation of carriers from quantum dots core onto the surface states, respectively [38]. A schematic representation of different dynamic relaxation processes is shown in Fig. 4d, where S0, S1, and Ssur represent ground state, excited state of QDs core, and surface state, respectively. In our kinetic model, k
nrC is the nonradiative rate constant of QDs core, k
CS is the rate constant of the irreversible electronic transition from QDs core to surface states. As aforementioned results, the PL of QDs originates from surface states, thus we assumed that the radiative recombination of QDs core is neglectable and there is only the radiative recombination of surface state with rate constant of k
rS . The rate equations of the proposed kinetic model of MoS2 and WS2 QDs can be written as follows:
(1)
∂
N C
/ ∂ t =
G C
−
(
k
n r C
+
k
C S
)
N C
,
(2)
∂
N S
/ ∂ t =
G S
−
k
r S
N S
+
k
C S
N C
,
where N
C is the population of photoexcited electron concentration in QDs core (C) and N
S in the surface states (S), G
C and G
S represent the carrier generation rate in QDs core and surface states, respectively. In time-resolved PL, the QDs is excited by ~ ps laser pulse, so there is G
C = G
S = 0. For simplicity, let us consider that the initial carrier concentrations of QDs core and surface states are equal, N
0 . And we could solve rate equation (1) and (2):
(3)

The ensemble averaged decay curve of QDs is given by I(t) = N

where I
0 is initial emission intensity. We have fitted the decay curves at detection wavelength of 405 nm using equation (5) and the values of the parameters k
rS
, k
nrC
, and k
CS are determined. The fitted curves are presented in Fig. 4c, and the values of parameters are given in Table 2 . The rate constants of MoS2 QDs are very close to the corresponding rate in WS2 QDs. The radiative recombination rate of surface state (k
rS ) is much larger than the transition rate from QDs core to surface states (k
CS ). Furthermore, the transition rates (k
CS ) in both QDs gradually decrease with increasing excitation wavelength from 273 to 343 nm that result in the dominance of fast process in transient PL at long wavelength excitation. In contrast, the nonradiative rates of QDs core (k
nrC ) increase with the increase of excitation wavelength. This can be explained by the size-dependence of rate constants due to quantum confinement effects [8,39]. The size of QDs is distributed; therefore larger QDs with higher value of nonradiative recombination rate might be excited at long wavelengths, resulting in the increase of nonradiative rate of QDs core (k
nrC ). The physical basis of carrier dynamic in QDs is complex that might include several nonradiative relaxation processes including carrier trapping at defect states, quenching due to local environment, relaxation from trap states, and so on [40]. Therefore further study using other spectroscopic technologies is needed to evaluate the whole picture about their carrier dynamics.

In conclusion, MoS2 and WS2 QDs with a narrow size distribution, intense PL emission, and excellent photostability were synthesized by femtosecond laser ablation in DEA solution. The PLQY of the as-prepared MoS2 and WS2 QDs reach a value up to 22.5%. The intense PL in these QDs is attributed to the emission of the surface state that created by abundant surface functional groups of QDs. The outstanding PL properties of MoS2 and WS2 QDs are great potential for the optoelectronic and bioimaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported the by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61690221, and 11674260), the Key research and development program of Shaanxi province (Grant No. 2017ZDXM-GY-120), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. xjj2017189), and the collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology. The TEM work was performed at the International Center for Dielectric Research (ICDR), Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, China. The authors also thank Mr. Ma and Ms. Lu for their help in using TEM.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal dichalcogenide nanosheets: preparation, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Huang, Z. Zeng, H. Zhang, Metal dichalcogenide nanosheets: Preparation, properties and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev., 42 (2013) 1934-1946.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemistry of two-dimensional layered transition metal dichalcogenide nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Chhowalla, H.S. Shin, G. Eda, L.J. Li, K.P. Loh, H. Zhang, The chemistry of two-dimensional layered transition metal dichalcogenide nanosheets, Nat. Chem., 5 (2013) 263-275.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronics and optoelectronics of two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.H. Wang, K. Kalantar-Zadeh, A. Kis, J.N. Coleman, M.S. Strano, Electronics and optoelectronics of two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides, Nat. Nanotechnol., 7 (2012) 699-712.
A. Splendiani, L. Sun, Y. Zhang, T. Li, J. Kim, C.Y. Chim, G. Galli, F. Wang, Emerging photoluminescence in monolayer MoS2, Nano Lett., 10 (2010) 1271-1275.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atomically thin MoS2: a new direct-gap semiconductor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.F. Mak, C. Lee, J. Hone, J. Shan, T.F. Heinz, Atomically thin MoS2: A new direct-gap semiconductor, Phys. Rev. Lett., 105 (2010) 136805.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-unity photoluminescence quantum yield in MoS2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Amani, D.H. Lien, D. Kiriya, J.X.A. Azcatl, J. Noh, S.R. Madhvapathy, R. Addou, S. KC, M. Dubey, K. Cho, R.M. Wallace, S.C. Lee, J.H. He, J.W.A. III, X. Zhang, E. Yablonovitch, A. Javey, Near-unity photoluminescence quantum yield in MoS2, Science, 350 (2015) 1065-1068.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of luminescent monolayered tungsten dichalcogenides quantum dots with giant spin-valley coupling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Lin, Y. Xu, S. Zhang, I.M. Ross, A.C. Ong, D.A. Allwood, Fabrication of luminescent monolayered tungsten dichalcogenides quantum dots with giant spin-valley coupling, ACS Nano, 7 (2013) 8214-8223.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots derived from two-dimensional materials and their applications for catalysis and energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, G. Sun, N. Li, P. Chen, Quantum dots derived from two-dimensional materials and their applications for catalysis and energy, Chem. Soc. Rev., 45 (2016) 2239-2262.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-pot, facile, and versatile synthesis of monolayer MoS2/WS2 quantum dots as bioimaging probes and efficient electrocatalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction
